name: Tropical & Subtropical Moist Broadleaf Forests
keyCharacteristics: Dense, multi-layered canopy with year-round warmth and rainfall exceeding 2000mm annually. Highest terrestrial biodiversity on Earth.
conservationStatus: Over 50% of original extent lost to deforestation. Losing approximately 10 million hectares per year globally. IUCN considers tropical moist forests among the most threatened biomes.
description:
Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests, commonly known as tropical rainforests, are the most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystems on Earth. Found near the equator where temperatures remain warm year-round and rainfall is abundant, these forests support an estimated 50-80% of all terrestrial species.

The canopy structure is complex, with emergent trees reaching 60-70 meters, a main canopy at 30-45 meters, an understory layer, and a forest floor that receives less than 2% of sunlight. Epiphytes, lianas, and other climbing plants are abundant.

Major regions include the Amazon Basin, Congo Basin, and Southeast Asian archipelago. These forests play a crucial role in global carbon cycling, storing an estimated 250 billion tonnes of carbon.
